title: Acute Food Shortage — Govandi, Mumbai
text: Approximately 80 families (≈ 340 individuals) in Govandi East slum cluster, M-Ward, Mumbai are facing acute food insecurity following mass layoffs in the local recycling and informal labour sector. Field survey conducted 12 April 2026 by Seva Sanstha volunteers confirms 35 children under age 5 are showing early signs of moderate acute malnutrition (MUAC below 12.5 cm). 22 lactating mothers have reported skipping meals for 3+ consecutive days. Existing dry ration stocks at the community distribution point were exhausted 4 days ago with no resupply scheduled. The local ration shop under PDS is non-operational due to card verification issues. Immediate requirements: 80 household dry ration kits (10 kg rice, 3 kg dal, 1 L oil, salt, spices); RUTF (Ready-to-Use Therapeutic Food) for 35 malnourished children; baby food packets for 12 infants under 6 months. Volunteers needed: 10 food distribution workers, 2 nutritionists for MUAC re-screening. Situation is critical — deterioration expected within 72 hours without intervention. Contact: Ms. Rekha Patil, Seva Sanstha Govandi, +91-9820XXXXXX.

title: Mobile Medical Camp Needed — Khardi Village, Thane
text: Village Panchayat of Khardi, Thane district reports a critical healthcare access gap affecting 250+ residents. The nearest Primary Health Centre is 18 km away via a road that becomes impassable during monsoon. Of the affected population, 40% are elderly (60+), 15 are pregnant women (3 high-risk with gestational hypertension), and 22 children under 5 are overdue for routine immunisation. A local healthcare worker (ASHA) has flagged 4 cases of suspected dengue and 6 cases of unmanaged Type-2 diabetes with no insulin supply since January. The village has no cold chain facility for vaccine storage. Requesting: mobile medical unit deployment for 2 full days; team composition — 1 general physician, 1 nurse-midwife, 1 lab technician (for blood glucose and dengue rapid tests); essential medicines kit (antihypertensives, ORS, antibiotics, wound care supplies); 20 dosages of childhood vaccines. Preferred window: within 5 days. Local coordination contact: Mr. Ganesh Ware, Gram Panchayat Secretary, Khardi.

title: Post-Flood School Closure — Barpeta District, Assam
text: Following the July 2024 monsoon floods that submerged 28 districts of Assam, three government primary schools in Simlabari, Gopalpur, and Haibargaon villages of Barpeta district remain completely non-operational 6 weeks post-flood. A combined student population of 420 children (ages 6–14; 52% girls) has received zero formal education during this period. Physical damage: roof collapse in Simlabari School (Grade 1–5), mud floor destruction in Gopalpur and Haibargaon schools, all textbooks and learning materials lost to floodwater. Mid-Day Meal kitchens are non-functional, affecting nutrition for 380 children who depend on the meal programme. Teachers report children displaying signs of psychosocial stress. Immediate needs: 3 weatherproof temporary learning tents (20 ft x 40 ft); 420 student kits (notebook, pencils, eraser, schoolbag); temporary blackboards; 6 volunteer teachers for minimum 4-week engagement; 1 trained child psychologist for group sessions. Contact: Block Education Officer, Barpeta, and local NGO Pratham Assam chapter.

title: Landslide Displacement — Mundakkai, Wayanad
text: A series of landslides triggered by extreme rainfall on 30 July 2024 struck Mundakkai, Chooralmala, and Noolpuzha villages in Wayanad district, Kerala. As of 1 August, 65 families (approximately 290 people) have been displaced from their homes; 4 villages remain partially cut off from road access. Current shelter: 241 individuals are in 2 government relief camps (Mundakkai UP School, Vythiri Community Hall); 49 individuals are staying with relatives. Most urgent needs: 290 cooked meals twice daily (special provision for 45 children under 10 and 8 elderly bedridden individuals); 65 waterproof shelter tarpaulins (12x15 ft); 70 hygiene kits (sanitary napkins, soap, ORS sachets, toothbrushes); 10,000 litres of clean drinking water per day for 3 days; 5 portable water purifiers for sustained supply. Medical: 2 paramedics for on-site first aid; 1 mental health counsellor. Logistics: road partially restored — relief vehicles can reach up to 2 km from camp site; mule convoy needed for last mile. Requesting 20 trained disaster-response volunteers for 72-hour deployment. Contact: District Collector Office, Wayanad + Caritas India Field Coordinator.

title: Borewell Failure, Water Crisis — Bagoda, Barmer
text: Gram Panchayat of Bagoda, Barmer district, Rajasthan reports a complete drinking water failure affecting 600 residents across 3 hamlets. The sole community borewell — installed under Jal Jeevan Mission in 2021 — has been non-functional for 11 consecutive days due to a submersible pump motor failure. Replacement parts have not arrived from Jodhpur. In the interim, women and children (primarily girls) are walking an average of 4.2 km twice daily to a pond whose water tests positive for coliform bacteria (E. coli confirmed by NGO Gramalaya field test). In the past 10 days: 3 hospitalised cases of acute diarrhoea (all children under 8); 7 cases of gastroenteritis reported at anganwadi. High-risk groups: 90 children under 10, 12 pregnant women, 45 elderly residents. Immediate requirements: 1 qualified borewell technician + 1 pump mechanic for repair (estimated 1–2 days); water purification tablets (500-tablet stock for 150 households, 2-week supply); 4 large-capacity polythene water storage tanks (2,000 L each); 1 water tanker for 3 days as interim supply. Contact: Sarpanch Ramji Bhai Bishnoi, Bagoda Gram Panchayat.

title: Seasonal Food Scarcity, Tribal Area — Koraput, Odisha
text: A coalition of 120 tribal families (approx. 540 individuals) from the Kondh community in 4 hamlets of Lamtaput block, Koraput district, Odisha are experiencing severe seasonal food scarcity following crop failure due to erratic monsoon (40% deficit in rainfall during kharif season). PDS ration cards for 68 families remain non-updated in the government database despite applications filed in March 2025, denying them legal entitlement to subsidised food. MUAC screening conducted by Gram Vikas field workers on 22 March 2026 found 28 children (ages 6 months–5 years) with measurements below 12 cm (severe acute malnutrition) and 41 in the moderate acute malnutrition range. Adult women show signs of iron deficiency anaemia. The community's traditional food stores (kodo millet, forest produce) are completely exhausted. Requirements: 120 monthly food baskets (7 kg rice, 2 kg dal, 500 g salt, 1 L oil per basket); iron and folic acid supplement tablets for 80 adult women; MUAC screening and treatment support for 69 malnourished children; legal aid volunteer to liaise with district collector office for emergency ration card issuance. Verify with: Gram Vikas field officer, Koraput cluster.

title: Maternal Health Emergency — Sitapur, Uttar Pradesh
location: Milkipur Village, Sitapur District, Uttar Pradesh
text: ASHA worker Smt. Kamla Devi of Milkipur village, Sitapur district, UP has escalated an urgent maternal health alert. 18 pregnant women are at or past their expected delivery dates with no safe delivery facility within reachable distance. District Women's Hospital is 22 km away; no government ambulance is available at night (the sole 108-service vehicle is shared across 14 villages). Of the 18 women, 4 are categorised as high-risk: 2 with gestational diabetes requiring insulin (supply disrupted since February), 1 with severe anaemia (Hb 6.2 g/dL), and 1 with pre-eclampsia showing BP of 160/105. A home birth resulted in a neonatal death 3 weeks prior in the same cluster. Urgent requirements: 2 trained nurse-midwives for residential 10-day camp deployment; basic delivery kit supplies (sterile gloves, cord clamps, oxytocin vials, IV sets, gauze); insulin (30 units/day x 2 patients x 10 days); iron infusion supplies for anaemia patient; coordination with 108 ambulance service for guaranteed night-time cover. Also needed: 1 volunteer female community health worker for follow-up home visits. Time-sensitive — 3 women may deliver within 5 days. Contact: Block Medical Officer, Sitapur.

title: Mid-Day Meal Disruption — Gaya, Bihar
text: The PM-POSHAN Mid-Day Meal Programme in 5 government primary schools (Ambedkar PS, Gandhi PS, Netaji PS, Nehru Vidyalaya, Kasturba Girls School) in Fatehpur block, Gaya district, Bihar has been suspended for 3 consecutive weeks due to a funding gap at the implementing NGO partner. A combined student population of 1,100 children (ages 6–14; 58% girls, predominantly Scheduled Caste and EWS families) have been deprived of their primary midday meal, which for many is their only nutritious meal of the day. School attendance has declined from an average 82% to 31% since the suspension. 4 cooking staff have not received salaries for 6 weeks. Verified by Block Education Resource Centre (BERC) field visit on 10 April 2026. Immediate requirements: ₹1.8 lakh emergency operational fund OR in-kind food supplies equivalent (1,100 meals/day x 21 days); 15 volunteer cooks (3 per school); 1 nutritionist for 2-day menu review and recertification; NGO coordination support to process delayed government reimbursement. Food stocks needed: 1,200 kg rice, 300 kg dal, 80 L cooking oil, spices, vegetables. Contact: BERC Coordinator, Fatehpur Block, Gaya.

title: Cloudburst Road Damage, Village Cut Off — Kullu, HP
location: Raison Village, Kullu District, Himachal Pradesh
text: A cloudburst event on 25 July 2024 destroyed the only motorable road connecting Raison village, Kullu district, HP to the main National Highway-3. The road collapse spans approximately 80 metres and has rendered the village of 340 residents (12 households) completely inaccessible by vehicle for 9 days. Two individuals requiring urgent evacuation: (1) Meera Devi, 28 years, 8-month pregnant, last antenatal check 7 weeks ago; (2) Ram Prakash Thakur, 67, with history of myocardial infarction, has been without cardiac medication for 4 days. Village food supplies estimated at 5–7 days. Mule trail remains open — estimated 4-hour trek. Weather forecast indicates 48-hour window before next heavy rain. Critical requirements: logistics volunteers with Himalayan terrain experience (minimum 8 persons); 2 paramedics for medical triage and evacuation support; 7-day food and medicine supply for 12 households via mule convoy; coordination with HP NDRF team deployed at Mandi; emergency helicopter evacuation requisition for 2 critical patients. Also required: civil engineer assessment for temporary road repair feasibility. Contact: Pradhan, Raison Gram Panchayat, +91-9816XXXXXX.

title: Urban Flood Aftermath — Velachery, Chennai
text: Ward 47, Velachery, Chennai experienced severe inundation following Cyclone Michaung-linked heavy rainfall (46.8 cm in 48 hours). As of Day 9 post-flooding, 180 low-income families (approx. 720 individuals) in the Vivekananda Nagar tenement complex remain without electricity, functional toilets, and clean water. The ground floor has 2 feet of residual stagnant water harbouring mosquito breeding grounds — dengue risk is elevated (7 suspected cases in the cluster). 65% of residents are daily wage workers who have lost 9 days of income (estimated average loss: ₹3,600/household). 200+ children under 12 are out of school, and anganwadi on premises is non-operational. Detailed needs assessment: (1) Logistics: 5 electric pumps for de-waterlogging, fuel for 3 days; 8 sanitation volunteers for waste clearance. (2) Food: 180 dry ration kits + 720 cooked meals for 2 days. (3) WASH: 2 portable water purifiers; 180 hygiene kits; 5 temporary toilet units. (4) Health: 2 nurses for dengue screening and dengue rapid test kits (×200). (5) Livelihood: emergency cash support coordination (₹2,000/family via CSR partner). Contact: RWA President Mr. Rajan Pillai, +91-9841XXXXXX; Coordinator: Greater Chennai Corporation Ward Welfare Officer.
